does: This paper introduces SkillVetBench, a leaderboard system that uses LLM-as-Judge evaluation to assess security risks in modular skills for open-source LLM agents. The core contribution is SARS (Skill Agentic Risk Score), a five-dimensional metric specifically designed to evaluate instruction-following systems across multiple risk vectors. The system integrates CVSS v4.0 vector decomposition and provides a dual-view interface comparing LLM-generated security reviews with official marketplace verdicts. Rather than relying on traditional code-layer scanning, this approach addresses semantic and instruction-layer threats such as prompt injection, data exfiltration through encoded channels, and harm chains across multi-agent pipelines. The work demonstrates that the LLM-as-Judge framework achieves high detection rates for agentic security risks.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about security vetting in decentralized LLM agent ecosystems, extending beyond traditional static code analysis tools that cannot capture instruction-layer attacks or multi-agent coordination risks. It responds to limitations in conventional security scanners like SAST and DAST tools, which operate at the syntactic level and miss semantic vulnerabilities unique to agentic systems. The paper connects to prior work on LLM safety and adversarial evaluation, particularly research on prompt injection, indirect prompt injection, and jailbreaking—natural-language attack vectors that code-only analysis cannot detect. It also incorporates established security frameworks (CVSS) adapted for the agentic context, building on risk assessment methodologies from traditional cybersecurity while innovating metrics specific to agent skill definitions and tool composition threats.
enables: This framework enables systematic vetting of community-contributed agent skills at scale, establishing a public infrastructure for continuous security evaluation in open-source LLM agent marketplaces. By providing both quantitative risk scores and detailed LLM-generated explanations, it supports developers in remediating vulnerabilities and helps users make informed decisions about skill adoption. The leaderboard format encourages security improvements through transparency and comparison. Future work can extend this approach to multi-agent interaction risk assessment, develop specialized detectors for specific attack patterns in agentic systems, and refine the dimensional risk metric based on real-world exploitation data. This foundation could enable automated security gates in skill deployment pipelines and inform the design of more robust agent architectures that constrain dangerous instruction-following behaviors.
